Tool Call: retrieve("TTC stain myocardial infarction")

Retrieved Documents: 32 documents

Based on the provided evidence, the myocardium affected by myocardial infarction indeed appears pale or white upon application of triphenyltetrazolium chloride (TTC) because the dead cells no longer contain active mitochondria capable of reducing TTC into formazan, thus failing to absorb the dye. Healthy cardiomyocytes maintain mitochondrial function allowing them to reduce TTC to produce a brick-red coloration. Since the infarcted areas lose this ability, they remain unstained appearing white compared to surrounding tissues that show the typical red/pink coloration indicative of live tissue taking up the TTC. raw_response:
Tool Call: retrieve("TTC stain myocardial infarction")

Retrieved Documents: 32 documents

Based on the provided evidence, the myocardium affected by myocardial infarction indeed appears pale or white upon application of triphenyltetrazolium chloride (TTC) because the dead cells no longer contain active mitochondria capable of reducing TTC into formazan, thus failing to absorb the dye. Healthy cardiomyocytes maintain mitochondrial function allowing them to reduce TTC to produce a brick-red coloration. Since the infarcted areas lose this ability, they remain unstained appearing white compared to surrounding tissues that show the typical red/pink coloration indicative of live tissue taking up the TTC. Thus, I concur with the initial assessment - the correct answer is:
\boxed{A} White.
This confirms the earlier reasoning about TTC staining reflecting differences in viability rather than just anatomical boundaries between regions. The detailed descriptions of how TTC interacts biochemically with living vs dead tissue support this interpretation strongly. No new reasons arise to change our original position given the comprehensive nature of the gathered evidence regarding TTC's use in diagnosing MI through differential staining effects.
